title: Sensory Awareness, Step by Step Find a comfortable position. Staying in this position, listen to the questions below, listening for your response after each question. If you do not have a recording of these questions, you can make one for yourself (or ask a friend to make one), recording each question with about 5 seconds

pages.text:
337
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Distress Tolerance Handout 9a
Sensory Awareness, Step by Step
Find a comfortable position. Staying in this position, listen to the questions below, listening for your
response after each question. If you do not have a recording of these questions, you can make
one for yourself (or ask a friend to make one), recording each question with about 5 seconds
between each question. 1.	Can you feel your hair touching your head? 2.	Can you feel your belly rising and falling as you breathe? 3.	Can you feel the space between your eyes? 4.	Can you feel the distance between your ears? 5.	Can you feel your breath touching the back of your eyes while you inhale? 6.	Can you picture something far away? 7.	Can you notice your arms touching your body? 8.	Can you feel the bottoms of your feet? 9.	Can you imagine a beautiful day at the beach?
10.	Can you notice the space within your mouth?
11. Can you notice the position of your tongue in your mouth?
12.	Can you feel a breeze against your cheek?
13.	Can you feel how one arm is heavier than the other?
14.	Can you feel a tingling or numbness in one hand?
15.	Can you feel how one arm is more relaxed than the other?
16.	Can you feel a change in the temperature in the air around you?
17. Can you feel how your left arm is warmer than the right?
18.	Can you imagine how it would feel to be a rag doll?
19.	Can you notice any tightness in your left forearm?
20.	Can you imagine something very pleasant?
21.	Can you imagine what it would feel like to float on a cloud?
22.	Can you imagine what it would feel like to be stuck in molasses?
23.	Can you picture something far away?
24.	Can you feel a heaviness in your legs?
25.	Can you imagine floating in warm water?
26.	Can you notice your body hanging on your bones?
27.	Can you allow yourself to drift lazily?
28.	Can you feel your face getting soft?
29.	Can you imagine a beautiful flower?
30.	Can you feel how one arm and leg are heavier than the other?
Note. Items 29 and 30 are adapted from Goldfried, M. R., & Davison, G. C. (1976). Clinical behavior therapy. New York: Holt, Rinehart & Win-
ston. Copyright 1976 by Marvin R. Goldfried and Gerald C. Davison. Adapted by permission of the authors.

Distress Tolerance Handout 9a
Sensory Awareness, Step by Step
Find a comfortable position. Staying in this position, listen to the questions below, listening for your
response after each question. If you do not have a recording of these questions, you can make
one for yourself (or ask a friend to make one), recording each question with about 5 seconds
between each question. 1.	Can you feel your hair touching your head? 2.	Can you feel your belly rising and falling as you breathe? 3.	Can you feel the space between your eyes? 4.	Can you feel the distance between your ears? 5.	Can you feel your breath touching the back of your eyes while you inhale? 6.	Can you picture something far away? 7.	Can you notice your arms touching your body? 8.	Can you feel the bottoms of your feet? 9.	Can you imagine a beautiful day at the beach?
10.	Can you notice the space within your mouth?
11. Can you notice the position of your tongue in your mouth?
12.	Can you feel a breeze against your cheek?
13.	Can you feel how one arm is heavier than the other?
14.	Can you feel a tingling or numbness in one hand?
15.	Can you feel how one arm is more relaxed than the other?
16.	Can you feel a change in the temperature in the air around you?
17. Can you feel how your left arm is warmer than the right?
18.	Can you imagine how it would feel to be a rag doll?
19.	Can you notice any tightness in your left forearm?
20.	Can you imagine something very pleasant?
21.	Can you imagine what it would feel like to float on a cloud?
22.	Can you imagine what it would feel like to be stuck in molasses?
23.	Can you picture something far away?
24.	Can you feel a heaviness in your legs?
25.	Can you imagine floating in warm water?
26.	Can you notice your body hanging on your bones?
27.	Can you allow yourself to drift lazily?
28.	Can you feel your face getting soft?
29.	Can you imagine a beautiful flower?
30.	Can you feel how one arm and leg are heavier than the other?
Note. Items 29 and 30 are adapted from Goldfried, M. R., & Davison, G. C. (1976). Clinical behavior therapy. New York: Holt, Rinehart & Win-

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Sensory Awareness, Step by Step Find a comfortable position. Staying in this position, listen to the questions below, listening for your response after each question. If you do not have a recording of these questions, you can make one for yourself (or ask a friend to make one), recording each question with about 5 seconds

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Choose one sentence or tool that fits today.
Use the low-spoon version; no full worksheet is required.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
